exemplar.title:
n What Ways does Brewing Time Variation (1 Minute
Variation) Affect the Physical, Chemical, and Antioxidant Properties of Teas (1.00 gram), Considering Changes in Gallic Acid Concentration, pH, and Colour?

detailedExemplarData.title:
n What Ways does Brewing Time Variation (1 Minute
Variation) Affect the Physical, Chemical, and Antioxidant Properties of Teas (1.00 gram), Considering Changes in Gallic Acid Concentration, pH, and Colour?

strengths: Clear and concise research question tightly linked to experimental design
Wellâstructured variable tables with uncertainties supporting transparency
Coherent narrative maintaining focus on brewing time throughout
weaknesses: Occasional digressions into cultural context disrupt focus
Minor lapses in clarity (misplaced parentheses, blurred figures)
Scope stretched by inclusion of five tea types, leading to uneven depth
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The strandâlevel feedback consistently placed Focus and Method in the 3â4 band: two strands at 4 (topic communication and research question) and one at 3 (methodology). While the investigation is coherent and focused, minor digressions and methodological flaws prevent a higher band. A holistic score of 4/6 reflects strong overall focus with room for refinement.

strengths: Regular citation of key literature on tea polyphenols and health impacts
Clear application of titration stoichiometry and uncertainty treatment in sample calculations
Accurate use of chemical terminology (e.g., âpolyphenolsâ, âmolarityâ) in most contexts
weaknesses: Some figures lack formal inâtext citations and full references
Occasional terminological lapses (e.g., âcartilageâ for cuvettes, mixing concentration/volume)
Explanations of antioxidant capacity remain descriptive rather than mechanistic
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands of Knowledge and Understanding were placed squarely in the 3â4 band, with consistent use of sources and terminology but uneven rigor in citation and occasional errors. A score of 3/6 accurately reflects clear but not fully consistent knowledge application.

strengths: Substantial and wellâorganized dataset (5 teas Ã 5 times Ã 3 trials across three methods) directly addresses the research question
Clear identification of limitations (storage oxidation, temperature control) and qualitative evaluation of their impact
Evidence of critical thinking in proposing alternative explanations (temperature decomposition, oxidation)
weaknesses: Evaluation remains largely qualitative with limited quantitative error analysis
Statistical treatment (e.g., regression parameters, standard deviations) is absent
Mechanistic justification for some hypotheses (pH shifts) could be deepened
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The three strands of Critical Thinking fell in the 4â6 band (research quality = 5, analysis depth = 4, discussion and evaluation = 4). The overall performance shows good research relevance, clear analysis, and partially critical evaluation. Awarding 6/12 positions the work at the top of the midâband, recognizing its substantial strengths while noting the missing quantitative rigor.

strengths: Comprehensive structure following IB conventions (title page, abstract, TOC, sections, appendices)
Consistent layout (word count, page numbers, captions, APA references) supporting readability
weaknesses: Minor misalignments in table borders and occasional lowâresolution figures
Some duplicated headings and missing section numbering disrupt flow
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Presentation met all IB requirements with a clearly appropriate structure and correct layout, meriting the maximum 4/4. The minor formatting issues are outweighed by the overall professional and navigable presentation.

strandSelections.reasoning: The essay follows IB conventions: title page with word count, abstract-like intro, table of contents, sequential sections (Introduction, Methodology & Raw Data, Processing, Conclusion, References, Appendices).  Headings are appropriate and numbering is logical.  Minor structural issues include a missing Section 4, duplicated headings, and occasional long blocks of raw tables that disrupt flow.  All required elements are present, so the structure is âclearly appropriateââ3 marks within the 3-4 band.

The student presents a clearly titled investigation with a concise abstract-style introduction that outlines purpose, variables, and context. Background on tea chemistry and antioxidant relevance is sufficiently detailed to show why gallic-acid, pH, and colour are meaningful measures. While a few sentences are repetitive and some figures are blurred, the overall narrative is coherent and maintains focus on brewing time throughout 40 pages. Minor lapses in clarity (e.g., misplaced parentheses, occasional digressions into cultural aspects) prevent a fully polished, highly effective communication, but the work comfortably exceeds the adequacy threshold.

The student processes data into graphs, calculates slopes and comments on trends versus hypothesis.  Sample uncertainty propagation is shown but not extended to all datasets; statistical treatment (e.g., standard deviation, regression parameters) is missing.  Explanations for observed decreases invoke plausible chemical degradation but are not supported by additional experiments.  Conclusions are partly supported; therefore analysis is âclearâ but not âwell-supportedâ enough for the top of the band, warranting 4 marks.

The investigation collects a substantial data set: 5 teas Ã 5 times Ã 3 trials for three separate measurements, providing raw tables and processed summaries.  Methods align directly with the RQ, and uncertainties are recorded.  Weaknesses include lack of repeat brews for temperature control, potential oxidation artefacts, and limited justification of chosen analytical wavelengths.  The majority of research remains pertinent, so the strand fits mid-band (4-6), best represented by 5 marks.

The student demonstrates sound grasp of titration stoichiometry, uncertainty, pH theory and colourimetry basics, applying correct formulas and units.  However, chemical reasoning is occasionally inconsistent (e.g., equating decreasing pH solely to polyphenol breakdown despite alkaline initial pH values; mis-typing mol dm-3 values). The explanation of antioxidant capacity is descriptive rather than mechanistic.  Overall knowledge is âclear with partially effective use of sourcesâ.

Chemical terminology such as âgallic acidâ, âpolyphenolsâ, âtitration volumeâ, âmolarityâ, âuncertaintyâ and wavelength units is largely accurate and used consistently in tables and text.  Occasional lapsesâe.g., referring to colourimeter cuvettes as âcartilageâ, spelling errors, or mixing concentration and volume terminologyâlimit consistency.  Hence performance is solid but not fully consistent.

The student designs three complementary experiments (titration, pH probe, colourimetry) and provides full variable tables, apparatus lists and appendices.  Controls such as tea mass, water volume and NaOH concentration are identified, and calibration steps are described.  Nevertheless, the method has conceptual flaws: using 100 Â°C for all teas likely degrades gallic acid, and storing aliquots for later tests introduces uncontrolled oxidation. Source selection for procedures is limited mainly to standard lab practice, with little justification of chosen wavelengths or titrant concentration.  This shows âgenerally relevantâ but not âclearly appropriateâ methodology.

The student supplies a word count, page numbers, consistent font, and tables/figures with captions.  Table of contents hyperlinks appear functional, and APA-style references are formatted.  Some images are low-resolution, and a few table borders misalign, but overall layout supports easy navigation and meets IB formal presentation requirements; thus it earns 3 marks within the top band.

The discussion revisits each hypothesis element, notes where data contradict expectations and proposes alternative explanations (temperature decomposition, oxidation).  Limitations such as storage conditions and uncontrolled temperature are acknowledged, and improvements are suggested.  Evaluation, however, stays mostly qualitative; it omits quantitative error impact and does not compare findings with literature values.  This fits the descriptor âgood discussion with partially critical evaluationâ.

Literature on tea polyphenols, pH effects and gallic-acid health impact is cited regularly, and key equations are referenced.  Citations appear both in-text and in a full reference list.  Yet many figures lack clear source attribution, some data-processing steps (e.g., uncertainty propagation beyond one example) rely on general chemistry knowledge rather than specific sources, and the discussion of temperature degradation cites only one article.  The application is therefore âmostly relevantâ but not uniformly rigorous.

A single research question is explicitly stated twice and is specific: the effect of 1-minute variations in brewing time (1â5 min) on gallic-acid concentration, pH and colour of 1.00 g tea samples. The wording is unambiguous and clearly linked to the subsequent data collection and graphs.  However, the inclusion of five different tea types stretches the scope and means not all discussion is equally deep; focus drifts when temperature decomposition is introduced but not investigated. Thus the question is âclearly stated but only partially focusedâ, fitting the 3-4 band.

annotations.comment: The title clearly states the investigationâs focus on brewing time and tea properties, providing a concise, descriptive overview of variables (gallic acid, pH, colour).
The research question is explicitly stated and unambiguous, linking brewing time variations to gallic acid concentration, pH and colour measurements. This establishes strong focus.
Word count is provided on the title page, meeting IB formatting requirements and enhancing transparency.
The table of contents is comprehensive but overly detailed; consider consolidating repeated âRaw Dataâ entries or moving bulk tables to appendices to improve navigability.
Page numbers appear consistently in headers, which supports navigation and meets presentation conventions.
Figure 2âs caption mentions a source but lacks a formal in-text citation and date. Include full reference details to strengthen application of source material.
The historical and cultural context in the introduction is engaging but digresses from the research focus; streamline to maintain coherence with the central question.
Figure 1 lacks a descriptive caption explaining how the highlighted functional group relates to the titration mechanism. Add detail to improve clarity.
The variable table on page 7 thoroughly outlines independent, dependent and control variables with uncertainties, supporting methodological transparency.
Control variable âTemperature of Waterâ is annotated with <math>100^{\circ}</math>C inline. For consistency, ensure uniform unit formatting across cells.
Raw data tables are exhaustive and include uncertainties, but their volume disrupts flow. Consider moving detailed data to appendices.
Decimal places in raw data are inconsistent (e.g., â2.10â vs â1.90â). Standardize to two decimal places throughout for uniform presentation.
Including uncertainties with each absorbance reading demonstrates careful attention to measurement precision. This strengthens data reliability.
The inline use of â&â in mathematical expressions introduces formatting inconsistencies. Use LaTeX syntax (e.g., \&) to ensure clarity.
The hypothesis in Section 2.1 lacks mechanistic justification for expected pH changes. Expand chemical reasoning to link antioxidant extraction with pH shift.
There is a stray parenthesis in the research question formulation (â(1) minute variation)â). Correct punctuation to improve readability and precision.
The experimental design clearly identifies controls, variables and methods for the three analyses, demonstrating thorough planning.
Equation 10âs relative uncertainty formatting uses percent sign inconsistently; present as a separate calculation step for clarity.
Final reported concentration \(C=(0.021\pm2.898\times10^{-4})\,\mathrm{mol\,dm^{-3}}\) correctly includes uncertainty; ensure units are uniformly formatted.
The LaTeX presentation of the pH equation is accurate and enhances the clarity of theoretical discussion.
The pH methodology omits duration of probe calibration and equilibration time. Specify these to improve reproducibility.
The pH data table clearly shows averages with uncertainties, indicating rigorous data processing.
Chamomile teaâs average pH at 4 min (7.27) deviates from trend. Discuss potential systematic error or contamination.
Colourimetry introduction clearly defines concepts and cites appropriate sources, demonstrating solid understanding.
Variables table for colourimetry is comprehensive and properly details controls and uncertainties.
The extensive wavelength absorbance data provide depth but lack statistical analysis; consider summarizing key wavelengths with trends.
Sample calculation for moles and uncertainties is thorough, demonstrating clear application of titration chemistry.
The discussion links high-temperature decomposition of gallic acid to data trends, showing critical evaluation and engagement with literature.
Reference to âtemperature and gallic acidâ in Figure 10 description is a typo; it should mention brewing time. Correct label to avoid confusion.
Conclusion summarizes outcomes well but would benefit from quantitative comparison of key findings (e.g., slopes, error impact) to strengthen evaluation.
